<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable assignments, source definitions pointing to official GitHub releases with pinned version tags and valid SHA-256 checksums, and a `package()` function. There is no `pkgver()` function, no command substitutions or dangerous commands (e.g., eval, curl, base64) in the global scope, and no code that executes during sourcing. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only fetch the source archives and parse the PKGBUILD, which is completely safe. The package function is not executed by these commands.
</details>
<summary>
Safe – no malicious code in global scope or pkgver().</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/nerva-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-v1_ugnap...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-v1_ugnap
Analyzing nerva-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-v1_ugnap) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines two source URLs for x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, both pointing to GitHub releases under the praetorian-inc/nerva repository. The sources are direct downloads of versioned tarballs from the project's official release page, using HTTPS. The URLs are constructed from variables, but all components are statically defined within the PKGBUILD and point to expected, legitimate locations. There is no command substitution, dynamic fetching from external inputs, or execution of downloaded content during sourcing.

The `pkgver` is hardcoded as "1.21.0", so no `pkgver()` function is present, and thus no runtime version detection logic is executed. The only executable portions in the global scope are variable assignments, which are safe. The `sha256sums` are provided and match the respective architecture-specific sources. No `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` steps are run due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, and even if they were, they fall outside the scope of this narrow gate.

Therefore, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will safely fetch the declared release binaries over HTTPS without executing any malicious code or performing unintended network operations.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source URLs and no dynamic execution
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source URLs and no dynamic execution[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches pre-built binaries from the project's official GitHub repository (praetorian-inc/nerva) under the expected release structure. The sources are architecture-specific tarballs for x86_64 and aarch64, each with a corresponding fixed sha256 checksum, indicating checksums were verified at packaging time. The download URLs follow the standard GitHub releases format and point to a tagged release version (v1.21.0), ensuring immutability.

The package() function performs standard operations: installing the binary to /usr/bin, and documentation/LICENSE to standard system directories. There are no signs of obfuscated code, unauthorized network requests, or dangerous commands. All operations are consistent with packaging a binary release from upstream.

The source URLs and checksums are correctly scoped to the declared upstream project. No dynamic fetching, git operations at build time, or execution of downloaded scripts is present. The package does not modify or access sensitive system areas beyond its own file installation. No privacy-invasive or malicious behaviors are evident.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package from trusted upstream
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is the standard Apache License 2.0 legal text, which is a permissive open-source software license. It contains no executable code, scripts, or commands of any kind. The content consists solely of license terms, definitions, and a boilerplate notice for applying the license to software projects.

There are no signs of malicious content, obfuscated code, network operations, file manipulations, or any other potentially harmful behavior. The presence of URLs (e.g., http://www.apache.org/licenses/) is standard and expected within this license. No `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, or other suspicious commands are present, as this is purely a legal document.

This file poses no security risk and is entirely consistent with standard open-source licensing practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Apache License file, no executable code.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml, src/README.md...
+ Reviewed src/LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- Standard Apache License file, no executable code.
LLM auditresponse for README.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a README.md for the nerva-bin package, hosted on GitHub under the praetorian-inc organization. It contains documentation for a Go-based network service fingerprinting tool called Nerva, including installation instructions, usage examples, and protocol support details.

The content is consistent with standard project documentation and describes expected behavior for a network reconnaissance tool. It explains how to install the tool via go install, build from source, or use Docker. The usage examples demonstrate legitimate security assessment functionality such as service detection, protocol fingerprinting, and misconfiguration detection—all of which are standard capabilities for authorized security testing tools.

There are no signs of malicious code, obfuscated commands, unauthorized data exfiltration, or harmful build/install procedures. The network operations described (port scanning, service probing) are within the expected scope of the tool's stated purpose. The project appears to be a legitimate open-source security tool licensed under Apache 2.0, with transparent development practices including public issue tracking and contributions from multiple authors.

No supply chain attack vectors or malicious behaviors were detected in the documentation.
</details>
<summary>
Benign security tool documentation with no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml...
+ Reviewed src/README.md. Status: SAFE -- Benign security tool documentation with no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration for nvchecker, a tool used to monitor upstream sources for new releases. This file specifies that the `nerva-bin` package should track the latest release from the GitHub repository `praetorian-inc/nerva`, using version tags with a `v` prefix. This behavior is consistent with standard version tracking practices in AUR packages and poses no security risk.

There are no network requests, executable commands, or system modifications defined in this file. It does not execute code, download binaries, or access sensitive data. The configuration only informs nvchecker where and how to check for version updates, which is a normal and expected use of the tool. The source is clearly attributed to the official repository of the project.

While the file does not pin to a specific commit or release (which is normal for version-checking configurations), it uses the legitimate upstream source for the project. There is no obfuscation, unexpected behavior, or malicious indicators present.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for version tracking; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>
